body.question_1: How would you rate the technical competency of the student intern in their relevant field of study?
body.answer_1: The intern from the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program showed fair technical competency, with a solid theoretical foundation that required additional scaffolding to translate effectively into professional practice. Core technical knowledge in the primary discipline areas was present and generally accurate, enabling the intern to engage meaningfully with assigned tasks after an initial onboarding period. However, familiarity with specific industry-standard tools and processes was limited, requiring time investment from senior staff to bridge the gap. Stronger emphasis on applied tool training and industry-facing projects within the curriculum would produce more immediately effective graduates and reduce the onboarding burden on host companies.

body.question_2: How well did the intern demonstrate professional communication and interpersonal skills in the workplace?
body.answer_2: This intern's professionalism was moderate, showing genuine commitment and a positive attitude that formed a solid foundation, with some areas requiring coaching and development during the placement period. Reliability and punctuality were good overall, though proactive communication about progress and obstacles was inconsistent and required encouragement from supervisors. The intern engaged positively with team members and accepted feedback constructively in most situations, demonstrating the willingness to learn that is essential for professional growth. Additional preparation in formal workplace communication — particularly written reporting standards and stakeholder update practices — would improve the professional impact of graduates from this program.

body.question_3: How effectively did the intern collaborate with team members and contribute to group projects?
body.answer_3: The intern's ability to collaborate effectively within a team environment was inconsistent and required more management attention than anticipated. Contribution to group tasks was often uneven — the intern sometimes worked in isolation rather than coordinating with colleagues, leading to duplicated effort and avoidable misalignments in shared workstreams. Communication within the team was reactive rather than proactive, and the intern occasionally struggled with the shared accountability and compromise that effective team collaboration demands. We recommend the School of Information Technology incorporate more structured team-based project work and collaborative simulation into the Computer Science curriculum to better prepare students for the realities of modern workplace team dynamics.

body.question_4: How would you assess the intern's problem-solving abilities and approach to challenging or unfamiliar tasks?
body.answer_4: The intern's problem-solving abilities were among the most excellent attributes they brought to our organization from the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program. When confronted with unfamiliar challenges or technical obstacles, the intern approached them systematically — breaking problems into manageable components, forming and testing hypotheses, and escalating appropriately only when genuinely blocked by limitations beyond their current capability. Their intellectual curiosity, persistence under pressure, and willingness to explore unconventional solutions made them a genuinely valuable problem-solver within the team. This quality is difficult to teach in the workplace and speaks highly of the critical thinking culture embedded in the School of Information Technology's academic programs.

body.question_5: How quickly and effectively did the intern adapt to your company's work culture, tools, and environment?
body.answer_5: The intern's adaptability to our workplace culture, processes, and evolving project requirements was superb and demonstrated a level of professional resilience and flexibility that we consistently value in permanent hires. Changes to project scope, team structure, and delivery timelines were managed with composure, and the intern made adjustments to their work approach quickly and effectively without requiring extended support or reassurance from management. They were proactive in seeking to understand the context behind changes rather than simply executing instructions, which allowed them to contribute meaningfully even in fluid project environments. This quality reflects a well-rounded education at the School of Information Technology that extends beyond technical training to genuine professional agility.

body.question_6: How proficient was the intern in English communication, both written and verbal, for professional purposes?
body.answer_6: The intern's English communication proficiency — both written and spoken — was remarkable and met the professional standards required for effective participation in our bilingual team environment. Written reports, emails, and documentation were consistently clear, professionally structured, and free of errors that would compromise understanding or credibility in a client-facing context. Verbal communication in meetings and collaborative sessions was confident and coherent, enabling full participation in discussions with both local and international colleagues. The intern's English proficiency reflects well on the language preparation embedded in the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program and is a strong asset in our increasingly international work environment.

body.question_7: How well did the intern's academic training align with the practical demands and standards of your industry?
body.answer_7: Our experience with this intern has highlighted inconsistent gaps between what the School of Information Technology's Computer Science curriculum delivers and what the professional industry actually requires from graduates in this field. Specifically, the intern lacked working proficiency in several tools and methodologies that are now industry standard, requiring us to invest significant onboarding time in areas that should be covered academically. Soft skills — particularly professional communication, project management, and stakeholder management — also appeared underdeveloped relative to what professional roles at this level require from day one. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with the School of Information Technology's curriculum design team to provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement.

body.question_8: What specific technical or soft skill areas do you believe the university curriculum should strengthen for future graduates?
body.answer_8: Based on our experience with this intern from the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program, we are commendablely impressed with the alignment between the academic curriculum and the practical demands of our industry. The foundational knowledge, theoretical frameworks, and applied skills the intern brought to the role mapped closely to what we require in graduate-level professional practitioners in this field. In particular, the project-based and problem-solving components of the curriculum appear to have translated well into workplace readiness. We would encourage the School of Information Technology to continue developing its industry connections and to involve practitioners actively in curriculum review to maintain this strong alignment as the professional landscape continues to evolve.

body.question_9: How would you rate the intern's overall performance and value contribution to your organization during the placement?
body.answer_9: Overall, the intern's performance during this placement was substandard and fell below the standard we expected based on the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program reputation. While the intern showed genuine enthusiasm and willingness to engage with assigned tasks, output quality was inconsistent, deadlines were occasionally missed without adequate advance communication, and independent contribution to team goals was limited relative to the support investment required from supervisors. We encourage the School of Information Technology to strengthen the preparatory components of its internship program — including pre-placement coaching and more rigorous selection criteria — to better match student readiness with the expectations of professional host organizations.

body.question_10: Based on your experience with this intern, would you consider hiring from this university in the future, and why?
body.answer_10: Based on this intern's performance and the overall quality of graduates we have observed from the School of Information Technology's Computer Science program, we would absolutely consider hiring from this institution in the future — both for internships and for permanent graduate positions. The intern's technical competency, professional attitude, collaborative skills, and growth mindset align closely with the profile we seek when making graduate hiring decisions. We are actively discussing a return internship offer for this individual and have already flagged the School of Information Technology as a preferred graduate source institution for our talent acquisition team. We would encourage the School of Information Technology to continue deepening its industry partnerships and look forward to an ongoing and mutually beneficial relationship with the institution in the years ahead.
